title: Mantı
subtitle: Turkish lamb dumplings with garlic yoghurt and spiced butter
excerpt: Tiny Turkish dumplings filled with spiced lamb mince, boiled and served on a pool of cold garlic yoghurt with hot paprika butter poured over and dried mint and sumac scattered across. The contrast of hot, cold, sour, and fatty is the whole point. Time-consuming to make but worth the effort.
sourceNotes: Mantı has roots in Central Asian and Mongolian dumpling traditions (manti, buuz, jiaozi sharing common Turkic and Mongolian origins) and entered Anatolian cooking during the Seljuk and Ottoman periods. The dish is documented in Ottoman culinary literature and regional Turkish cookbooks from the nineteenth century. Regional versions vary: Kayseri mantı are famously tiny; Boş mantı (empty dumplings) appear in some regions. The garlic yoghurt and spiced butter dressing is consistent across most Anatolian versions.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze assembled, uncooked dumplings on a tray until solid, then transfer to bags. Cook from frozen, adding 3 to 4 minutes to the boiling time.
recipe.batchNotes: Make a double batch of dumplings and freeze half uncooked.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assembled, uncooked dumplings can be refrigerated for up to 4 hours before boiling.

content.text: Mantı is a family cooking project. Making the dumplings takes time — the traditional Kayseri style demands dumplings small enough that forty fit in a tablespoon, which is a meaningful claim about how small they should be. A more practical home size is about 3 cm square. Smaller than wontons, larger than the Kayseri ideal, still very good.

The filling is tiny and very simply seasoned: lamb mince, grated onion, salt, and black pepper. Nothing else — the flavour comes from the dressing rather than the filling. The garlic yoghurt must be cold and generously seasoned. The paprika butter must be poured over sizzling. The mint and sumac go on last, scattered over everything.

Combine the {{plain-flour}} of flour, {{eggs}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt in a bowl. Mix and then knead for 8 minutes until smooth and elastic. If the dough is sticky, add flour a tablespoon at a time. Wrap in cling film and rest at room temperature for 30 minutes.

Grate the {{onion}} onion finely and squeeze out all liquid. Combine with the {{lamb-mince}}, {{black-pepper}} of pepper, and a generous pinch of salt. Mix well. The filling should be very lightly seasoned — it is raw, so taste a tiny cooked piece in a pan to check the seasoning.

On a floured surface, roll the dough very thin — 1 to 2 mm. Cut into 3 cm squares. Place a small amount of filling (about half a teaspoon) in the centre of each square. Bring the four corners together over the filling and pinch firmly to seal, forming a small parcel. Place on a floured tray and cover with a damp cloth while you work through the remaining dough.

Beat the {{garlic}} of garlic into the cold {{plain-yoghurt}} of yoghurt and season well with salt. Spread onto four serving plates or into a wide bowl. Bring a large pot of generously salted water to a boil. Add the mantı and cook for 8 to 10 minutes until the dough is tender and the filling is cooked through. Drain and immediately pile onto the yoghurt. In a small pan, melt the {{unsalted-butter}} until it foams and turns golden. Add the {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika and swirl. Pour sizzling over the mantı. Scatter the {{mint-dried}} of dried mint and {{sumac}} of sumac over. Serve immediately.

items.symptom: Dumplings burst and the filling escapes during boiling
items.cause: Dough not sealed firmly, or dough too thin in places
items.fix: Press the sealed edges very firmly; ensure the dough thickness is even throughout

items.symptom: Dough is thick and gummy rather than silky
items.cause: Dough not rolled thin enough; 1 to 2 mm is much thinner than it seems
items.fix: Keep rolling until the dough is thin enough to see your hand through it

items.cause: Seasoning in raw lamb difficult to judge
items.fix: Fry a small piece of filling in a pan and taste before assembling all the dumplings

items.symptom: Yoghurt curdled when the hot dumplings landed on it
items.cause: Yoghurt was at room temperature, or low-fat yoghurt used
items.fix: Keep the yoghurt refrigerated until the last moment; use full-fat yoghurt only, which is more stable

content.text: Mantı traces its lineage through Central Asia, where similar dumpling traditions (manti in Turkish and Kazakh, buuz in Mongolian, jiaozi in Chinese) share common nomadic roots and the practical logic of wrapping meat in portable dough. It entered Anatolia with Turkic migrations and became entrenched in Ottoman palace cooking, documented in imperial kitchen records from the fifteenth century. Today it is particularly associated with Kayseri in central Turkey, where the competitive standard is how small you can make the dumplings. The defining dressing (cold garlic yoghurt, hot paprika butter, dried mint, and sumac) is the same across the region and is what makes mantı distinctly Turkish rather than simply a dumpling.
